focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

At the stone table in the innermost room, farther from the blocked outer corridor, the coupling pattern was almost visible. Gavin had been staring at the same three lines of notation for the better part of an hour, the manuscript's representational shifts moving in slow cycles that his translation framework could only approximate, and he thought he was beginning to see it — not understand it, not yet, but see it. The ward-maintenance cycle and the substrate's ground state shared something. A resonance. A harmonic. The Builders' notation for this was dense and layered, showing relationships that collapsed when he tried to render them in the runic shorthand Babbling had given him, but the shape of the thing was there. The wards reached into the substrate's base grammar and synchronized with it, and when that synchronization held, the wards regenerated. Or reinforced. Or something the Builders had a glyph for that mapped onto no English verb he knew.

He was reaching for his quill to make a note when the substrate screamed.

Not screamed — there was no sound. But the perceptual channel the First Art had opened in his cognition, the one that made the substrate feel as immediate as heat on skin, convulsed with input. Hundreds of spells, all at once. Combat magic. The specific churn of offensive and defensive castings meeting, rebounding, shattering, recasting. Trying to read through it was like holding a book up to a lamp at point-blank range — not painful, exactly, but obliterating.

He gripped the edge of the stone table. The stone was cold under his fingers — it was always cold down here, the sub-collection's deep limestone chill that never warmed regardless of the season — and he held on to the solidity of it while his substrate perception convulsed. The de...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...anslation layer his mind was using to fake understanding. He only knew the manuscript had been written for a reader to whom this problem would have felt natural, and nothing about Gavin Tench had ever felt natural in that direction. He sat with the page until the back of his neck cramped and his nose dripped onto the stone between his shoes. Then he kept going anyway, because discouragement was only useful if there was another person to hand the work off to, and there wasn't.

Gavin turned a page. The notation shifted, showing the coupling mechanism from a new angle, and something in the relationship between the first two variables caught at the edge of the third, and he leaned forward, his nose bleeding onto the stone floor, and tried to understand how a long-departed civilization had solved a problem he was only beginning to know how to ask.

Students slept in dormitories behind stone walls, inside a castle inside a country inside a world inside a thinning shell of ancient agreements, and none of them knew how thin the shell had become, or that the only person working on it was a fifteen-year-old Hufflepuff with a nosebleed and bad handwriting, sitting on a cold floor, choosing the world over the war.

Over the next few days, the damage in the castle stopped looking temporary. A trophy case shattered and stayed shattered. Windows cracked and stayed cracked. Corridors that had looked ordinary that morning showed broken glass and scorch marks by supper. Gavin noticed that too, with the ordinary part of him and the part that now read the substrate underneath everything. He kept working, bad handwriting on borrowed parchment, blood drying on his sleeve, trying to hold three interlocking variables in one human mind while the war above him gathered itself for the last push.
